We are requesting your help again in correcting and 
updating our biennial report on State campaign finances laws. 
This report is one of our best sellers, and I hope it 
has reduced somewhat the information demands on your office 
-- or at least improved the quality of the questions. 
Even though this is a busy season for all of us, we 
would appreciate it if you could take a few minutes to review 
the enclosed materials and respond to our contractor. 
As always, we are grateful for your help in these things 
and we will be sending you a copy of Campaign Finance Law 
88 as quickly as the contractor receives and integrates your

Dear Mr. Larson: 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is now being prepared for publication this spring by the 
Federal Election Commission's National Clearinghouse on Election Administration. 
james A. Palmer and I again are assisting D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc., in 
the development of this compendium of state campaign finance laws for the 
Clearinghouse. 
This is our biennial request for your assistance and participation in 
the production of this important report. 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 will present the law on campaign finance regulation in 
each jurisdiction as of january 1, 1988, in two convenient formats: 
(1) a State Law 
Summary synopsizing the law and (2) Quick Reference Charts highlighting key 
provisions. 
The 1988 report will cover all major aspects of each jurisdiction's 
campaign finance law including: 
(1) 
Contribution and solicitation limitations. 
(2) 
Expenditure limitations. 
(3) 
Reporting requirements. 
(4) 
Tax and public-financing provisions. 
(5) 
Post-election requirements. 
(6) 
Offenses and penalties. 
(7) 
Responsible regulatory or supervisory state agencies. 
In order to produce a report with campaign finance law information for California 
that is as current, comprehensive, accurate, and useful as possible, we request that 
you; 
(1) Provide relevant printed materials that are available concerning California's 
campaign finance law and its interpretation and administration, such as (a) relevant 
statutes, administrative 
rules 
or 
regulations, and 
state 
constitutional 
provisions, 
(b) relevant advisory OpInIOnS or legal interpretations, (c) important and 
relevant 
state trial and appellate court and federal district court opinil?ns, and (d) and any 
other relevant guidance, including forms, instructions, 
summarIes of the 
law, and 
other campaign finance-related information, that will provide us with insight into the 
campaign finance law and its operation in California. 
Note: 
If you provided this 
information for CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86, we need only those printed materials that 
will bring us up to date as to any changes since january 1, 1986. 
(2) Review and critique the State Law Summary and Quick Reference Chart entries 
for California contained in CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86 (see enclosures). 
Please note on 
these documents (or in a separate letter if you prefer) any errors, omissions, changes 
since january 1, 
1986, and suggestions for improvement and return the marked-up 
copies. 
We realize that we have asked in the past and are again asking a lot of you and 
your staff, but it is the only way we can ensure that CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is a 
report that is acceptable to you and to us -- a product that is both useful and used 
and not one that creates more qUestions than it answers. 
Any assistance you may be 
able 
to 
provide 
us 
about 
California's 
campaign 
finance 
law 
will 
be 
greatly 
appreciated. 
We would be delighted if you could respond to this request within the 
next two weeks.

PART I 
QUICK REFERENCE CHARTS 
Qui ck-reference charts are presented in thi s part of the report and pro-
vide a convenient summary of the major provisions of state law concerning cam-
paign finance report filing requirements, contribution, solicitation, and ex-
penditure limitations, and public financing and special tax provisions in ef-
fect through December 31, 1985. 
More complete and detailed information on 
these topics for each jurisdiction is provided in Part II: 
Summaries of 
State Campaign Finance Law. 
Chart 1: 
Campaign Finance Report Filing Requirements. 
This chart 
presents information on campaign finance reporting requirements in each juris-
diction, with an emphasis on the requirements for reporting or disclosing cam-
paign-related contributions and expenditures. 
The chart identifies who is re-
quired to report ("required from"); the general contents for the reports 
("contents"); the time or schedule for filing the reports ("time"); the office 
or officer with whom the reports are required to be filed ("filed with"); ad-
ditional campaign finance-related reports that are required to be filed, such 
as a statement of organization or registration statement, the designation of a 
campaign treasurer, campaign depository, or principal campaign committee, and 
a report of independent expend i tures ("other report s or st atements requi red") ; 
and the state or local agencies responsible for the administration and en-
forcement of the campaign finance law in a jurisdiction ("regulatory agen-
cy"). 
Charts 2-A and 2-8: 
Contribution and Solicitation Limitations. 
These 
charts summarize the state law provisions concerning limitations on contribu-
tions and the solicitation of contributions. 
Contribution limitation infor-
mation includes the restrictions on contributions to particular recipients 
(e.g., candidates and political conrnittees) in particular types of campaigns 
or elections (e.g., primary or general election) made by particular types of 
contributors: 
individuals, candidates, family members of candidates, corpora-
tions, labor unions, separate segregated funds (PACs), banks and savings and 
loan institutions, regulated industries, governmental officers and employees, 
and political parties. 
Special restrictions on particular methods of making 
contributions are presented: cash contributions, anonymous contributions, and 
contributions made in the name of another. 
Limitations on solicitation by 
governmental officers and employees are also noted. 
Charts 3-A and 3-8: 
Expenditure Limitations. 
These charts synopsize 
the major provisions concerning election campaign-related expenditures, in-
cluding identification of the individuals who may authorize expenditures, the 
permissible ~ount of expenditures, and any restrictions on when expenditures 
may be made, as well as related reporting or filing requirements. 
The autho-
rized uses for which expenditures may be made, including the personal use of a 
candidate, his family, or others, transfer to other political committees, in-
vestment of campaign contributions and funds, and election day expenses, are 
arrayed. 
The law on alternative methods for making expenditues, such as by 
cash payment or on credit or by credit card, are also presented. 
A final cat-
egoray of "other restrictions" summarizes any special requirements concerning 
expenditures that are not listed in any other category.

PART II 
SUMMARIES OF STATE CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAWS 
Summari es of the campaign fi nance 1 aw in each state and the Di stri ct of 
Columbia are presented in this part of the report. 
Each summary is a synopsis 
of the state law in effect through December 31, 1985, concerning the following 
campaign finance-related subjects: 
• Contribution and solicitation limitations 
• Expenditure limitations 
• Reporting requirements 
• Pub 1 ic fi nanci ng provisions 
• Tax provisions 
• Post-election requirements 
• Offenses and penalties 
• Regulatory agenc i es 
The summaries include the most important requirements of the law as found 
in statutory codes and, if available, administrative codes, advisory opinions 
of the state regulatory agency, attorney general opinions, court decisions, 
and guidance manuals prepared by the state regulatory agency. 
Since each sum-
mary is current through December 31, 1985, and changes in state law after that 
date are not included, the reader is advised that it is essential that other 
sources be consulted for subsequent changes. 
It is suggested that the reader 
contact the state agency with responsibility for the administration and en-
forcement of the state's election campaign finance law (names, addresses, and 
telephone numbers are included in Chart 1 of the preceding part of the report) 
or review the most recent amendments to the state's statutory and administra-
tive codes. 
. 
The law concerning state and local elections is covered in each summary; 
however, state requirements for federal elections are also included to the ex-
tent that state law has not been superseded by the Federal Election Campaign 
Act of 1971, as amended. 
The provisions of this act and regulations of the 
Federal Election Commission supersede and preempt any provision of state law 
with respect to federal office, and the Federal Election Commission by regula-
tion has stated that the act supersedes state law concerning the organization 
and registration of political committees supporting federal office candidates, 
disclosure of receipts and expenditures regarding federal office candidates 
and political committees, and limitations on contributions and expenditures 
regarding federal office candidates and political committees. 
The act does 
not supersede state law providing for a candidate's personal financial disclo-
sure, and copi es of reports requi red to be fi 1 ed under the act must al so be 
filed either with the secretary of state of the state or with the state offi-
cer who is charged with maintaining state election ca~paign finance reports.

II. 
CONTRIBUTION AND SOLICITATION LIMITATIONS 
A. 
Definition of Contribution. 
A contribution is a payment, a forqive-
ness of a loan, a payment of a loan by a third party, or enforceable promise 
to make a payment, except to the extent that full and adequate consideration 
is received, unless it is clear from the surrounding circumstances that it is 
not made for political purposes [1]. 
An expenditure made at the behest of a 
candidate, committee, or elected officer is a contribut ion unless full and 
adeQuate consideration is received for making the expenditure [2]. 
A contri-
bution includes the purchase of tickets for events such as dinners, luncheons, 
rallies, and similar fundraising events; the candidate's own money or property 
used on behalf of his or her candidacy; the granting of discounts or rebates 
not extended to the publ ic generally or the 
~lranting of discounts or rebates 
by television and radio stations and newspapers not extended on an equal basis 
to all candidates for the same office; and the payment of compensation by any 
person for the personal services or expenses of any other person if such ser-
vices are rendered or expenses incurred on behalf of a candidate or committee 
without payment of full and adeguate consideration [3). 
A contribution also 
includes a transfer of anything of value to a committee from another committee 
unless full and adequate consideration is received [4]. 
A contribution does not include an iJTlount received pursuant to an en-
forceable promise to the extent such iJTlounts have been previously reported as 
a contribution [5]. 
A contribution does not include a payment made by an oc-
cupant of a home or office for costs related to any meetinq or fundraisinq 
event held in the occupant's home or office if the costs for the meeting or 
fundralsing event are $500 or less [6J. 
A contribution also does not include 
volunteer personal services or a payment made by any individual for his own 
travel expenses If such payments are made voluntarily without any understand-
ing or agreement that they will be, directly, or indirectly, repaid [7].

Any person who knowingly or willfully violates any provision of the 
Political Reform Act is guilty of a misdemeanor [198].

times the amount the person failed to report properly or unlawfully contrib-

violation [199]. 
Criminal prosecution must be commenced within four years 
after the date on which the violation occurred [200]. 
No person convicted of 
a misdemeanor under the Political Reform Act may be a candidate for anyelec-
tive officer or act as a lobbyist for a period of four years following the 
date of the conviction unless the court at the time of sentencing specifically 
determines that this provision must not be applicable [201]. 
A plea of nolo 
contendere must be deemed a conviction for these purposes [202]. 
Any person 
who violates this section is guilty of a felony [203].

inadvertent, negligent, or deliberate, and the presence or absence of good 
faith must be considered in applying the permissible remedies and sanctions 
[204] •

PART I 
QUICK REFERENCE CHARTS 
Quick-reference charts are presented in thi s part of the report and pro-
vide a convenient summary of the major provisions of state law concerning cam-
paign finance report f11ing requirements, contribution, solicitation, and ex-
penditure limitations, and public financing and special tax provisions in ef-
fect through December 31, 1985. 
More complete and detailed information on 
these topics for each jurisdiction is provided 1n Part II: 
Sunlllaries of 
State CalPaign Finance Law. 
Chart 1: 
C_paign Finance Report Filing Requirements. 
This chart 
presents information on campaign finance reporting requirements in each juris-
diction, with an emphasis on the requirements for reporting or disclosing cam-
paign-related contributions and expenditures. 
The chart identifies who is re-
quired to report ("required from"); the general contents for the reports 
("contents"); the time or schedule for filing the reports t"time"); the office 
or officer with whom the reports are required to be filed ("filed with"); ad-
ditional campaign finance-related reports that are required to be filed, such 
as a statement of organization or registration statement, the designation of a 
campaign treasurer, campaign depository, or principal campaign committee, and 
a report of independent expenditures ("other reports or statements required"); 
and the state or local agencies responsible for the administration and en-
forcement of the campaign finance law in a jurisdiction ("regulatory agen-
cy"). 
Charts 2-A and 2-8: 
Contribution and Solicitation L1l11itations. 
These 
charts summarize the state law provisions concerning limitations on contribu-
t ions and the solicitation of contribut ions. 
Contribut ion limitation infor-
mation includes the restrictions on contributions to particular recipients 
(e.g., candidates and political cOlI'Inittees) in particular types of campaigns 
or elections (e.g., primary or general election) made by particular types of 
contributors: 
individuals, candidates, family members of candidates, corpora-
tions, labor unions, separate segregated funds (PACs), banks and savings and 
loan institutions, regulated industries, governmental officers and employees, 
and pol itical parties. 
Special restrictions on particular methods of making 
contributions are presented: 
cash contributions, anonymous contributions, and 
contributions made in the name of another. 
Limitations on solicitation by 
governmental officers and employees are also noted. 
Charts 3-A and 3-8: 
Expenditure L1mitat ions. 
These charts synopsi ze 
the major provisions concerning election campaign-related expenditures, in-
cluding identification of the individuals who may authorize expenditures, the 
permissible amount of expenditures, and any restrictions on when expenditures 
may be made, as well as related reporting or filing requirements. 
The autho-
rized uses for which expenditures may be made, including the personal use of a 
candidate, his family, or others, transfer to other political committees, in-
vestment of campaign contributions and funds, and election day expenses, are 
arrayed. 
The law on alternative methods for making expenditues, such as by 
cash payment or on credit or by credit card, are also presented. 
A final cat-
egoray of "other restrictions" summarizes any special requirements concerning 
expenditures that are not listed in any other category.

PART II 
SUMMARIES OF STATE CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAWS 
Summaries of the campaign finance law in each state and the District of 
Columbia are presented in this part of the report. 
Each summary is a synopsis 
of the state law in effect through December 31, 1985, concerning the following 
campaign finance-related subjects: 
• Contribution and solicitation limitations 
• 
Expenditure limitations 
• Reporting requirements 
• Public financing provisions 
• 
Tax provisions 
• Post-election requirements 
• Offenses and penalties 
• Regulatory agencies 
The summaries include the most important requirements of the law as found 
in statutory codes and, if available, administrative codes, advisory opinions 
of the state regulatory agency, attorney general opinions, court decisions, 
and guidance manuals prepared by the state regulatory agency. 
Since each sum-
mary is current through December 31, 1985, and changes in state law after that 
date are not included, the reader is advised that it is essential that other 
sources be consul ted for subsequent changes. 
It is suggested that the reader 
contact the state agency with responsibility for the administration and en-
forcement of the state's election campaign finance law (names, addresses, and 
telephone numbers are included in Chart 1 of the preceding part of the report) 
or review the most recent anendments to the state's statutory and administra-
tive codes. 
The law concerning state and local elections is covered in each summary; 
however, state requirements for federal elections are also included to the ex-
tent that state law has not been superseded by the Federal Election Campaign 
Act of 1971, as amended. 
The provisions of this act and regulations of the 
Federal Election Commission supersede and preempt any provision of state law 
with respect to federal office, and the Federal Election Commission by regula-
t ion has stated that the act supersedes state 1 aw concern ing the organi zat ion 
and registration of political committees supporting federal office candidates, 
disclosure of receipts and expenditures regarding federal office candidates 
and political committees, and limitations on contributions and expenditures 
regarding federal office candidates and pol itical committees. 
The act does 
not supersede state law providing for a candidate's personal financial disclo-
sure, and copi es of report s requi red to be fil ed under the act must al so be 
filed either with the secretary of state of the state or with the state offi-
cer who is charged with maintaining state election campaign finance reports.

Dear Mr. Larson: 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is now being prepared for publication this spring by the 
Federal Election Commission's National Clearinghouse on Election Administration. 
james A. Palmer and I again are assisting D.T. Skelton Service Associates, Inc., in 
the development of this compendium of state campaign finance laws for the 
Clearinghouse. 
This is our biennial request for your assistance and participation in 
the production of this important report. 
CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 will present the law on campaign finance regulation in 
each jurisdiction as of january 1, 1988, in two convenient formats: 
(1) a State Law
sections.conclusion:
synopsizing the law and (2) Quick Reference Charts highlighting key 
provisions. 
The 1988 report will cover all major aspects of each jurisdiction's 
campaign finance law including: 
(1) 
Contribution and solicitation limitations. 
(2) 
Expenditure limitations. 
(3) 
Reporting requirements. 
(4) 
Tax and public-financing provisions. 
(5) 
Post-election requirements. 
(6) 
Offenses and penalties. 
(7) 
Responsible regulatory or supervisory state agencies. 
In order to produce a report with campaign finance law information for California 
that is as current, comprehensive, accurate, and useful as possible, we request that 
you; 
(1) Provide relevant printed materials that are available concerning California's 
campaign finance law and its interpretation and administration, such as (a) relevant 
statutes, administrative 
rules 
or 
regulations, and 
state 
constitutional 
provisions, 
(b) relevant advisory OpInIOnS or legal interpretations, (c) important and 
relevant 
state trial and appellate court and federal district court opinil?ns, and (d) and any 
other relevant guidance, including forms, instructions, 
summarIes of the 
law, and 
other campaign finance-related information, that will provide us with insight into the 
campaign finance law and its operation in California. 
Note: 
If you provided this 
information for CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86, we need only those printed materials that 
will bring us up to date as to any changes since january 1, 1986. 
(2) Review and critique the State Law Summary and Quick Reference Chart entries 
for California contained in CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 86 (see enclosures). 
Please note on 
these documents (or in a separate letter if you prefer) any errors, omissions, changes 
since january 1, 
1986, and suggestions for improvement and return the marked-up 
copies. 
We realize that we have asked in the past and are again asking a lot of you and 
your staff, but it is the only way we can ensure that CAMPAIGN FINANCE LAW 88 is a 
report that is acceptable to you and to us -- a product that is both useful and used 
and not one that creates more qUestions than it answers. 
Any assistance you may be 
able 
to 
provide

law 
will

appreciated. 
We would be delighted if you could respond to this request within the 
next two weeks.

We have indicated the changes as you requested and they are shown on the enclosed copies. Where major changes are necessary, they are indicated by an insert number. That number corresponds to the number on the typed "Inserts" document, also enclosed. I trust that these materials adequately and accurately respond to your request. We look forward to receipt of your new publication, campaign Finance Law 88. If you have any questions regarding the enclosures, I suggest that you phone Ms. Jeanne Pritchard, Chief, Technical Assistance and Analysis Division. Her telephone number is (916) 322-5662.
